name.en-US: On the Perfection of the Spiritual Life

description.en-US: A defence of the religious state as a state of perfection, against the position of Gerard of Abbeville that perfection consists in the secular clerical life rather than in religious profession.
description.la: Defensio status religiosi ut status perfectionis, contra positionem Gerardi de Abbatisvilla quod perfectio in vita clericali saeculari potius quam in professione religiosa consistit.

sources.description: English translation by John Procter OP (Burns and Oates, London, 1902, PD), revised by the Aquinas Institute. Mirrored from aquinas.cc.
Latin text from the Leonine / Marietti editions (public domain), mirrored from aquinas.cc.

title.en-US: Chapter 1 — The author’s intention
Chapter 2 — That the perfection of the spiritual life is according to charity
Chapter 3 — Perfection is both the love of God and the love of our neighbor
Chapter 4 — The perfection of divine love in God
Chapter 5 — The perfection of divine love in those who have attained to beatitude
Chapter 6 — The perfection of divine love necessary to salvation
Chapter 7 — The perfection of divine love which is a matter of counsel
Chapter 8 — The first means of perfection which is the renunciation of earthly possessions
Chapter 9 — The second means of perfection which is the renunciation of earthly ties and of matrimony
Chapter 10 — Aids in the preservation of chastity
Chapter 11 — The third means of perfection which is the renunciation of our own will
Chapter 12 — These three means of perfection belong peculiarly to the religious state
Chapter 13 — A refutation of the errors against religious vows
Chapter 14 — The perfection of brotherly love which is necessary for salvation
Chapter 15 — The perfection of love of our neighbor considered as a matter of counsel
Chapter 16 — The perfection of brotherly love considered in its intensity
Chapter 17 — The perfection of brotherly love manifested by spiritual gifts
Chapter 18 — What is required to constitute the state of perfection
Chapter 19 — The state of perfection is a condition befitting bishops and religious
Chapter 20 — The episcopal office is more sacred than the religious life
Chapter 21 — Reply to objections against the perfection of the episcopal state
Chapter 22 — The dangers in desiring the episcopal office
Chapter 23 — Whether priests and archdeacons are in a state of higher perfection than religious
Chapter 24 — Other arguments used to show that parish priests are in a state of greater perfection than religious
Chapter 25 — Reasons why parish priests and archdeacons should not be placed in a more perfect state
Chapter 26 — The liability to suspension does not prove that parish priests or archdeacons are in a state of perfection
Chapter 27 — Refuting previous arguments about the higher degree of perfection in archdeacons and parish priests
Chapter 28 — Refuting the argument that the state of perfection is not hindered by a lack of a solemn blessing or consecration
Chapter 29 — Reply to arguments about the lack of hindrance to the state of perfection in those with the ability to resign duties
Chapter 30 — Concerning the works that a religious may lawfully undertake
